EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture streaming northward into the Mid Atlantic region combined with the approach of a cold front and low pressure, produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported on several roads across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture streaming northward into the Mid Atlantic region combined with the approach of a cold front and low pressure, produced heavy rain across much of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals generally ranged between two inches and seven inches across the county. Rainfall total of 6.42 inches was reported at (0.1 SW) South Mills. Rainfall total of 6.32 inches was reported at South Mills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals generally ranged between two inches and seven inches across the county. Rainfall total of 7.00 inches was reported at (1 NNE) Rockyhock. Rainfall total of 5.93 inches was reported at (13.3 NNW) Edenton. Rainfall total of 3.73 inches was reported at (0.4 NW) Edenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Eta moved from the eastern Gulf of Mexico, across the northern Florida peninsula, to off the South Carolina coast throughout the 11th and 12th. Tropical moisture streaming into the Carolinas throughout this time resulted in development of heavy rainfall, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts across the foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. This resulted in areas of flash flooding and main stem river flooding, some of which was significant, resulting in multiple fatalities and significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rainfall tapered off across the Charlotte area during the afternoon and evening, elevated flow conditions and some flooding persisted across many of the larger basins in the city through much of the evening. Numerous roads remained closed during this time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rainfall tapered off across the Catawba River basin during the afternoon and evening of the 12th, high water levels and elevated stream flows persisted in the area around Lookout Shoals dam during the overnight hours until the early morning of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of strong to severe storms developed during the late afternoon and evening in a hot and unstable summer time environment. A few of these storms became severe and produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a power line near Clayton Place and Church Circle Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds right in advance of low pressure and a cold front produced minor structural damage across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor damage was reported to several outbuildings and a gazebo near Camden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, draped across the foothills and Piedmont and supported aloft by multiple waves of energy entering from the Tennessee Valley, provided the triggers for  scattered slow-moving thunderstorms to develop during each afternoon across the mountains east of Interstate 81.  Deep tropical moisture was in place across the region in the wake of Tropical Storm Isaias.  In addition, strong daytime heating provided the instability to allow storms to produce heavy rain, 2-4 inch per hour rainfall rates at times, while light winds aloft made for slow storm movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home mesonet station recorded 4.46 inches of rain which fell in a little over 2 hours.  This equated to roughly a 150-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 point rainfall frequency analysis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the lower mid-Atlantic region during the period August 12th - 15th, with precipitable water values ranging from 1.6 to 1.9 inches each day.  A frontal boundary stalled near the Interstate 64 corridor provided a trigger for convection, supported aloft by passing upper level disturbances. Strong daytime heating contributed to deep instability, resulting in thunderstorms with high rainfall rates.  Light winds aloft resulted in mean wind speeds generally 10 knots or less, indicating slow cell movement. Considering the moist antecedent conditions, flash flood watches were in effect each day through this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal mesonet station recorded a storm total of 1.95 inches of rain.  1.89 inches fell within an hour, roughly a 5-year event per NOAA Atlas 14 point frequency estimates.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer in North Wilkesboro, NC recorded a 2-day total, for thunderstorms occurring during the afternoons on the 13th (2.89) and 14th (2.20), of 5.09 inches of rain.  This was the 8th wettest 2-day period for this station during the month of August, with station records dating back to September 1921.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms brought some flash flooding around the Kinston area. Some water rescues occurred and roads were flooded and impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some flash flooding on Stallings Rd was reported, amongst other areas around Kinston. Several water rescues occurred as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds aloft were responsible for carrying deep tropical moisture across the Piedmont during the morning of the 21st, while northeasterly flow near the surface was in place due to high pressure wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians. The high moisture content across the Piedmont, with a precipitable water value of 1.77��� measured from the morning sounding at KGSO, as well as very light mean winds, set the stage for slow-moving, highly efficient rainfall. This would be over soils that, in many areas, were 50-75 percent saturated to start the day due to earlier rainfall events per FLASH estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar estimated that between 2 to 4 inches fell from a thundestorm across much of the West Prong of the Little Yadkin River basin, much of this within a 2-hour period. One personal mesonet station recorded 6.18 inches storm total (suspect data, but not impossible). CREST unit streamflow across the basin ranged from 400/ to 700 cfs/mi^2, with average return intervals ranging from 50- to nearly 200-year events. This basin is 8.3 mi^2 in size across rural, modestly hilly land.  The West Prong of the Little Yadkin River overflowed its banks and flooded both Volunteer Road and Tillotson Road, making the roads impassible until the floodwaters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias originated from a vigorous tropical wave off the coast of Africa that was first identified by the National Hurricane Center on July 23, 2020. The tropical wave gradually became more organized, and became Tropical Storm Isaias on July 30. Isaias marked the earliest ninth named storm on record, surpassing 2005's Hurricane Irene by eight days. Isaias strengthened into a Category 1 hurricane the next day, reaching a peak intensity of 85 mph. On August 1, the storm made landfall on North Andros, Bahamas and subsequently weakened to a tropical storm before paralleling the east coast of Florida and Georgia. As it approached the Carolina coastline, it re-intensified to a hurricane shortly before making landfall near Ocean Isle Beach, North Carolina, at 11:10 PM EDT on August 3 as a Category 1 hurricane, and proceeded to accelerate up the East Coast of the United States. Across eastern North Carolina, Isaias produced tropical storm force winds, pockets of heavy rainfall, minor storm surge and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado associated with Hurricane Isaias formed just offshore over |the Pamlico River, and then moved over a small waterfront community off |of Rest Haven Road east of Bayview. The tornado snapped, twisted, or |uprooted numerous trees, the majority of them were pine trees. Several trees fell |partially on homes which caused damage to a few roofs, walls, and |vehicles. The tornado then continued northward, downing or snapping |several trees along Rest Haven Road. Then as the tornado reached NC 99, |several more trees were seriously damaged, snapped, or uprooted in the |vicinity of a few homes. There was also minor damage to a manufactured |home and a small shed had its roof blown off. After passing north of NC |99, the tornado continued to cause damage to the tops of several more |trees before dissipating in a large field.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado associated with Hurricane Isaias formed just offshore in the |Neuse River, and then moved over a small waterfront community in Kennel |Beach, with one large pine tree snapped that fell on a home along Pine |Road. The tornado produced additional tree damage along Live Oak Road |and Pecan Lane as it moved NNW towards swamps and forested lands. |Another area of snapped pine trees was found along a rural logging road |off South Goose Creek Road. The tornado then continued NW, producing an |area of enhanced tree damage to hardwoods and softwoods along the western |end of Goose Creek Road in Grantsboro. It should be noted that the tornado |likely lifted and descended during its path through western Pamlico County|before dissipating over swampy areas of Cypress Creek upstream of Goose |Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire dept reported part of a chicken house destroyed and numerous trees and a few power lines blown down at the intersection of Sulfur Springs Rd and Hiddenite Church Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms and storm clusters developed over western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. One cluster of storms produced damaging winds in Rutherford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported multiple trees and power lines blown down throughout the central part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms on the 24th developed in a typical summertime environment of deep southerly windflow, carrying tropical moisture across the mid-Atlantic (with daily precipitable water values ranging from 1.30 to 1.60 inches), while abundant sunshine each afternoon provided the instability (CAPE values 1500-2500 J/Kg each day). The results were spotty thunderstorms that were efficient in producing localized heavy rain, often over ground that was excessively moist from prior rainfall events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain upstream caused the Ararat River stream gauge at Ararat to rise from a stage of around 2 feet to a crest at around 11 feet in less than a 4-hour period, a within-bank rise to Action Stage for this gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias originated from a vigorous tropical wave off the coast of Africa that was first identified by the National Hurricane Center on July 23, 2020. The tropical wave gradually became more organized, and became Tropical Storm Isaias on July 30. Isaias marked the earliest ninth named storm on record, surpassing 2005's Hurricane Irene by eight days. Isaias strengthened into a Category 1 hurricane the next day, reaching a peak intensity of 85 mph. On August 1, the storm made landfall on North Andros, Bahamas and subsequently weakened to a tropical storm before paralleling the east coast of Florida and Georgia. As it approached the Carolina coastline, it re-intensified to a hurricane shortly before making landfall near Ocean Isle Beach, North Carolina, at 11:10 PM EDT on August 3 as a Category 1 hurricane, and proceeded to accelerate up the East Coast of the United States. Across eastern North Carolina, Isaias produced tropical storm force winds, pockets of heavy rainfall, minor storm surge and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some tropical storm force wind gusts affected the area during the overnight hours. A peak wind gust of 42 MPH was recorded at Oriental at 3:17 AM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, draped across the foothills and Piedmont and supported aloft by multiple waves of energy entering from the Tennessee Valley, provided the triggers for  scattered slow-moving thunderstorms to develop during each afternoon across the mountains east of Interstate 81.  Deep tropical moisture was in place across the region in the wake of Tropical Storm Isaias.  In addition, strong daytime heating provided the instability to allow storms to produce heavy rain, 2-4 inch per hour rainfall rates at times, while light winds aloft made for slow storm movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home mesonet station recorded a storm total 4.67 inches of rain.  Most of this fell in a 2-3 hour period, which equated to roughly a 150-year rainfall event during the 2 and 3 hour time periods per NOAA Atlas-14 point analysis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the lower mid-Atlantic region during the period August 12th - 15th, with precipitable water values ranging from 1.6 to 1.9 inches each day.  A frontal boundary stalled near the Interstate 64 corridor provided a trigger for convection, supported aloft by passing upper level disturbances. Strong daytime heating contributed to deep instability, resulting in thunderstorms with high rainfall rates.  Light winds aloft resulted in mean wind speeds generally 10 knots or less, indicating slow cell movement. Considering the moist antecedent conditions, flash flood watches were in effect each day through this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff due to heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused an unnamed creek to overflow its banks, and cause flooding of buildings nearby the intersection of 2nd Street and Statesville Road (Highway 115) in the town of North Wilkesboro.  The road reopened after flood waters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the lower mid-Atlantic region during the period August 12th - 15th, with precipitable water values ranging from 1.6 to 1.9 inches each day.  A frontal boundary stalled near the Interstate 64 corridor provided a trigger for convection, supported aloft by passing upper level disturbances. Strong daytime heating contributed to deep instability, resulting in thunderstorms with high rainfall rates.  Light winds aloft resulted in mean wind speeds generally 10 knots or less, indicating slow cell movement. Considering the moist antecedent conditions, flash flood watches were in effect each day through this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 5 inches of rain fell fell over a 2 to 3-hour period across nearly the entire drainage basin for Ramey Creek near the community of Lowgap.  Per FLASH data at the 3-hour period, these amounts were anywhere from 1.8 to 2.5 times flash flood guidance, or a 200+ year event.  CREST unit streamflows were over 1,000 cfs/mi^2, indicating a major flooding event was possible.  However, the area where the rain occurred is heavily wooded and very sparcely developed.  Given the late hour at which the rain occurred, there were very few eye witness accounts of what actually happened.  The only report received was early in the event, likely before maximum flooding was achieved, where over six inches of water was observed flowing across Ramey Creek Road due to flooding from Ramey Creek. This heavy rain also caused a small slide debris flow onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapo-transpiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer at Eden, NC (EDEN7) observed a 72-hour (ending 12Z on November 13th) precipitation amount of 3.37 inches. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Eta moved from the eastern Gulf of Mexico, across the northern Florida peninsula, to off the South Carolina coast throughout the 11th and 12th. Tropical moisture streaming into the Carolinas throughout this time resulted in development of heavy rainfall, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts across the foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. This resulted in areas of flash flooding and main stem river flooding, some of which was significant, resulting in multiple fatalities and significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the Yadkin River near Patterson exceeded its established flood stage after 4 to 7 inches fell across the basin, with much of that falling in only 3 hours or so between midnight and daybreak on the 12th. Multiple roads and some homes were inundated in the Happy Valley community, especially between Highway 268 and the river. Additionally, a few mudslides developed in the Yadkin basin, including a large one that covered/closed Kirby Mountain Rd north of Patterson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms on the 24th developed in a typical summertime environment of deep southerly windflow, carrying tropical moisture across the mid-Atlantic (with daily precipitable water values ranging from 1.30 to 1.60 inches), while abundant sunshine each afternoon provided the instability (CAPE values 1500-2500 J/Kg each day). The results were spotty thunderstorms that were efficient in producing localized heavy rain, often over ground that was excessively moist from prior rainfall events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar-estimated rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches were close to double that of the 1-hour flash flood guidance in the Joshua Creek basin.  The Wilkes County 911 Center reported that Joshua Creek washed out Long Bottom Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms on the 24th developed in a typical summertime environment of deep southerly windflow, carrying tropical moisture across the mid-Atlantic (with daily precipitable water values ranging from 1.30 to 1.60 inches), while abundant sunshine each afternoon provided the instability (CAPE values 1500-2500 J/Kg each day). The results were spotty thunderstorms that were efficient in producing localized heavy rain, often over ground that was excessively moist from prior rainfall events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 3 to 5 inch rainfall amounts from slow moving thunderstorms across northern Surry and southern Carroll Counties, with locally higher totals, were roughly double that of the 3-hour flash flood guidance.  CREST unit streamflows were anywhere from 600 cfs/ to over 1000 cfs/mi^2 along the NC/VA state line, with widespread areas of 100 year to 200+ year rainfall events.  Runoff from this rainfall resulting in flooding along Greenhill Road as Schoolhouse Creek overflowed its banks.  Brushy Fork was observed overflowing its banks, flooding White Pines Country Club Road.  Pauls Creek flooded out of its banks and onto both Pipers Gap Road and Busick Lane.  Much of Cross Creek County Club was underwater as Cross Creek rose out of its banks, destroying a pedestrian bridge and heavily damaging the course fairways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapo-transpiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple personal weather stations observed 3 to 5 inches of rain during the early morning hours of November 12th, about half of these amounts during a 90-minute period with the main convective band. This resulted in flash flooding of Forbush Creek, including along Lace Knoll Road where is crosses over the Creek. Lace Knoll Road was completely flooded and impassible until the flood waters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Eta moved from the eastern Gulf of Mexico, across the northern Florida peninsula, to off the South Carolina coast throughout the 11th and 12th. Tropical moisture streaming into the Carolinas throughout this time resulted in development of heavy rainfall, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts across the foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. This resulted in areas of flash flooding and main stem river flooding, some of which was significant, resulting in multiple fatalities and significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauges indicated flash flooding developed along numerous creeks throughout the Charlotte area after 3.5 to 4.5 inches of rain fell across much of the city, with the bulk of that falling in about a 3-hour period during the morning of the 12th. The most significant flooding occurred along the Sugar Creek and Little Sugar Creek basins, where several gauges reported moderate to even major flooding. Water from Sugar Creek inundated multiple buildings along Southern Pines Blvd and Red Oak Blvd. Little Sugar Creek overflowed and impacted several homes in the Parkstone neighborhood as well as several structures along Highway 51 in Pineville. On the opposite side of the city, significant flooding developed along Mallard Creek in the University City area, impacting homes on Dashiel Dr and Circle Dr. Irwin Creek also inundated Statesville Rd just north of Uptown. Steele Creek flooded in the Carowinds area, flooding several mobile homes along John Price Rd. Otherwise, multiple roads were inundated along Briar Creek and McMullen Creek in the east and southeast part of the city, and along Stewart Creek and Irwin Creek on the north side and near Uptown. Long Creek also flooded roads near Mountain Island Lake, while Clarks Creek overflowed and flooded multiple vehicles in the far north part of the city. Drainage flooding also developed along I-85N near Little Rock Rd, with multiple vehicles stranded and occupants rescued. Overall, 23 people were rescued from homes and 12 people from vehicles throughout the city.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported that poor drainage caused water to enter and damage a home on Dale Ave after 2 to 3 inches of rain fell across central Lincoln County in just a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of rain showers developing in the vicinity of a stationary front and in response to very moist upslope flow resulted in heavy rainfall along and near the southern Blue Ridge escarpment from the 12th into the early morning of the 13th, with as much as 4 inches of rain reported across portions of Transylvania County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the Davidson River indicated the river briefly exceeded established flood stage after 3 to 4 inches of rain fell over the basin in about 30 hours. At this stage, the river overflows its banks and inundates part of Davidson River Rd and Wilson Rd, while backwater effects into Turkey Creek cause Deavor Rd to be flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Sally moved into western North Carolina during the evening of the 16th and continued into the morning of the 17th. 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across much of the area in about 20 hours, resulting in minor flooding across the upper French Broad basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the Little River exceeded its established flood stage after 2 to 3 inches of rain fell throughout the basin in about a 20-hour period. The main impact was to lowlands along the stream and to Cascade Lake Rd, which was covered by more than a foot of water in spots. Minor river flooding also developed on the French Broad at Blantyre during the evening of the 17th and continued for almost 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense heat and humidity combined to produce heat index values of 110 degrees or higher during the afternoon hours of September 2nd and 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index peaked at around 111 degrees in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the Appalachians interacted with numerous westward propagating boundaries formed from morning convection along the East Coast. This boundary interaction was able to produce numerous storms, some of which reached severe limits, producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moving east brought damaging wind gusts in Walnut Cove. One tree was reported down from these winds on Easley Rd. A second tree was reported down along Main Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire dept reported trees blown down at County Home Rd and Lee Cline Rd on Herman Sipe Rd and on 1st Ave Place NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms and scattered storm clusters developed along a stationary front across western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. One storm produced brief damaging winds in Burke County. Locally heavy to excessive rainfall also occurred, resulting in isolated flash flooding in Catawba County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported trees and power lines blown down in the Drexel area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms and scattered storm clusters developed along a stationary front across western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. One storm produced brief damaging winds in Burke County. Locally heavy to excessive rainfall also occurred, resulting in isolated flash flooding in Catawba County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire dept reported brief flash flooding developed over southeast Catawba County after 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in a very short period of time. Highway 16 was inundated and impassable at Balls Creek Rd. Bandys Crossroad was flooded and closed at Sugar Farm Rd due to Battle Run overflowing its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed across western North Carolina throughout the afternoon and evening. Locally heavy to excessive rainfall developed, resulting in some flash flooding across McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency mngr reported flash flooding developed along Buck Creek Clear Creek and Locust Creek after 2.5 to 4.5 inches of rain fell across portions of western McDowell County in just a couple of hours. Locust Creek inundated Locust Cove Rd to depths of several feet near Jumping Branch Dr and damaged a vehicle. The bridge on Jumping Branch Rd was significantly damaged. Buck Creek flowed into a campground off Buck Creek Rd. Clear Creek overflowed a couple of bridges over Clear Creek Rd briefly cutting off access to homes. Several private bridges were also washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Winds associated with the tropical storm caused moderate (perhaps some locally major) tidal/coastal flooding across portions of northeast North Carolina north of the Albemarle and Currituck Sounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast winds associated with Tropical Storm Isaias resulted in moderate (perhaps some locally major) tidal flooding over portions of Pasquotank county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Eta moved from the eastern Gulf of Mexico, across the northern Florida peninsula, to off the South Carolina coast throughout the 11th and 12th. Tropical moisture streaming into the Carolinas throughout this time resulted in development of heavy rainfall, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts across the foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. This resulted in areas of flash flooding and main stem river flooding, some of which was significant, resulting in multiple fatalities and significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported flash flooding of a couple of streams in southern Cleveland County after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell, with much of that falling in about a three hour period during the morning of the 12th. Logan Branch inundated a portion of Sulphur Springs Rd and Buffalo Creek overflowed onto Cleveland Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, draped across the foothills and Piedmont and supported aloft by multiple waves of energy entering from the Tennessee Valley, provided the triggers for  scattered slow-moving thunderstorms to develop during each afternoon across the mountains east of Interstate 81.  Deep tropical moisture was in place across the region in the wake of Tropical Storm Isaias.  In addition, strong daytime heating provided the instability to allow storms to produce heavy rain, 2-4 inch per hour rainfall rates at times, while light winds aloft made for slow storm movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 3 to 4 inch rainfall amounts, with locally higher amounts in spots, across the town of Yadkinville and central portions of Yadkin County.  Most of this rain fell within a 3-hour timeframe.  Runoff from this rain caused numerous roads in the area to be flooded and impassible until the flood waters receded.  Among the roads that were flooded were Billy Reynolds Road and Booger Swamp Road in Yadkinville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm produced 4 to 5 inch rainfall amounts over a 2-hour period in the Fleetwood area.  CREST unit streamflow estimates put this event close to 1000 cfs/mi^2, or a 100-200 year rainfall event at both the 1- and 2-hour time intervals.  This resulted in several reports of flooding in the Fleetwood area, including at the intersection of Windy Hill Road and Highway 221, which made the intersection impassible.  The heavy rain caused a mudslide to occur along Paul Goodman Road.  Rock Quarry road was impassible where a tributary of the South Fork New River, the drainage area of which was completely within the area of heaviest rainfall, flooded out of its banks and over-topped the intersecting bridge.  The same tributary caused flooding along Dick Phillips Road further upstream where the two run adjacent to one another.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Cyclone Sally moved through the region during the evening and overnight, producing several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down over the Goose Creek Game Land along Highway 33 |over northwest Pamlico County, less than a mile from the Beaufort and |Pamlico County line and about 3.5 miles southwest of Lowland. ||In this remote location, a narrow path of debris could be found across|and along Highway 33. This consisted mainly of smaller size limbs of a |pine tree or other softwood tree. On the east side of Highway 33, 1 |large pine tree was snapped just above the base and another tree was |uprooted. The stronger damage was pointed toward the northeast, with |some lighter convergent damage toward the southeast or into the path of |the storm.||The pine tree that was snapped looked to have some disease and the|uprooted tree was in a very swampy area of the gameland, thus probably|had a lower threshold for uprooting. These two factors along with the |lack of any appreciable damage nearby was enough to rate the tornado on |the lower end of the scale or an EF0 with top winds of around 70 MPH.||Note, the path length could be much longer but due to the remote area|surveyed, it could not be followed for it's entirety.

Dense vegetation inhibited visibility at the start but the ending point is visible with pine trees snapped near the  land/marsh interface, with no damage visible across Jonaquin Creek just north of the ending location.||Path width was small, roughly 25 yards, with a length of 0.15 miles,|though as mentioned above, start point is estimated based on visible|tree damage from Periwinkle Court driveway. Maximum wind speeds are|estimated at 90-100 mph due to snapped pine and oak trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the lower mid-Atlantic region during the period August 12th - 15th, with precipitable water values ranging from 1.6 to 1.9 inches each day.  A frontal boundary stalled near the Interstate 64 corridor provided a trigger for convection, supported aloft by passing upper level disturbances. Strong daytime heating contributed to deep instability, resulting in thunderstorms with high rainfall rates.  Light winds aloft resulted in mean wind speeds generally 10 knots or less, indicating slow cell movement. Considering the moist antecedent conditions, flash flood watches were in effect each day through this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Doppler radar indicated rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 2 inches in the Stoney Fork area, with CREST unit streamflow indicating 550 cfs/mi^2.  FLASH data indicates this rainfall was roughly a 10-year event.  Runoff from this rainfall caused a creek running adjacent to Lawrence Greene Road to overflow its banks onto the roadway, making travel impossible until the water receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapo-transpiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer at Yadkin 6 E observed a 72-hour storm total of 5.70 inches rainfall, ending 12Z on November 13th.  This is the highest 72-hour total for the month of November in the station's history, though only the 19th-highest of all months.  The station observed a 24-hour total of 4.08 inches ending on 12Z of the 12th, and a 48-hour total of 5.15 inches ending at 12Z on the 13th, all of which are new highest records for the month of November.  These amounts are in the 5- to 10-year ARI range per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Frequency Estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias came off the African coast as a tropical wave on July 23, 2020. It became Tropical Storm Isaias on July 30th, further strengthening to a hurricane on the 31st. After crossing the Bahamas, it weakened to a tropical storm. Isaias strengthened again to a hurricane as it approached the coast of North Carolina. Hurricane Isaias made landfall at Ocean Isle Beach as a strong Category 1 around 11 pm on August 3rd, with an 87 mph recorded gust. It pushed inland to Goldsboro NC, where it weakened and accelerated northward. ||The hurricane spawned six tornadoes, ranging from EF-0 to EF-2. Most of the tornadoes occurred in Brunswick County.||Rainfall was heaviest along the coast of Georgetown County, topping out at nearly seven inches. Most areas along the coast of North and South Carolina received around three to four inches on average, with lesser amounts inland. The Wilmington ASOS reported a gust to 74 mph. Storm surge was significant in Brunswick County, where many docks were damage, and homes and businesses were inundated with three feet of water.  Downtown Wilmington also received a record surge that came up the Cape Fear River. Over 80,000 residences lost power in New Hanover county, with widespread outages along the coast of Pender County, as they were in the eastern eyewall. Total damage was at least 300 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado associated with Hurricane Isaias appeared to touch down |near the intersection of Andrew Jackson Highway (Highway 74) and |Ocean Highway East (Highway 17). This was characterized by a few |snapped pine trees. Additional trees were snapped along the path |near Old Fayetteville Road, Wayne Street NE, Village Road NE, and |Old Mill Road NE, although tree damage was spotty in nature.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dan River at Wentworth, NC (WENN7) was above flood stage (19 feet) from November 12th through the 13th, and crested at the moderate flood stage of 27.62 feet (38,500 cfs) at 6:30 AM EST on November 13th, the 5th highest crest on record for this gauge.  This was between a 25- and 50-year Average Return Interval per the USGS StreamStats website.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most significant flooding events in years occurred during November 11th and 12th, given both the amounts of rainfall and the footprint where flooding occurred.  In the wake of high pressure passing east off the mid-Atlantic coast, a plume of deep tropical moisture was rapidly carried inland to the central Appalachians ahead of a slow-moving cold front.  Though this plume was of tropical origin, it was not directly related to Tropical Storm Eta, which was off the coast of southern Florida.  Precipitable Water values were in the extremely high range of 1.9 to 2.2 inches with this airmass, around the 99th percentile for mid-November.  In addition, evapo-transpiration was significantly lower than during the previous few months given lower daytime heating and the fact that most vegetation had become dormant for the upcoming winter months.  Despite dry conditions for almost two weeks leading up to the event, numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding occurred as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across portions of the Piedmont on the 11th, followed by an additional 1 to 3 inches on the 12th.  Some of the activity was convective in nature, producing locally 2 to 4 inch per hour rainfall rates.  The rainfall produces numerous instances of flooding and flash flooding, followed shortly by river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yadkin River at Elkin, NC (ELKN7) was above flood stage (18 feet) on November 12th, and crested at the moderate flood stage of 21.81 feet (25,100 cfs) at 1:00 PM EST, the 8th highest crest on record for this gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture streaming northward into the Mid Atlantic region combined with the approach of a cold front and low pressure, produced heavy rain across much of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals generally ranged between two inches and seven inches across the county. Rainfall total of 7.01 inches was reported at Greens Cross.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias came off the African coast as a tropical wave on July 23, 2020. It became Tropical Storm Isaias on July 30th, further strengthening to a hurricane on the 31st. After crossing the Bahamas, it weakened to a tropical storm. Isaias strengthened again to a hurricane as it approached the coast of North Carolina. Hurricane Isaias made landfall at Ocean Isle Beach as a strong Category 1 around 11 pm on August 3rd, with an 87 mph recorded gust. It pushed inland to Goldsboro NC, where it weakened and accelerated northward. ||The hurricane spawned six tornadoes, ranging from EF-0 to EF-2. Most of the tornadoes occurred in Brunswick County.||Rainfall was heaviest along the coast of Georgetown County, topping out at nearly seven inches. Most areas along the coast of North and South Carolina received around three to four inches on average, with lesser amounts inland. The Wilmington ASOS reported a gust to 74 mph. Storm surge was significant in Brunswick County, where many docks were damage, and homes and businesses were inundated with three feet of water.  Downtown Wilmington also received a record surge that came up the Cape Fear River. Over 80,000 residences lost power in New Hanover county, with widespread outages along the coast of Pender County, as they were in the eastern eyewall. Total damage was at least 300 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Green Swamp Road and snapped|approximately 20 large pine trees on the west side, up to|20 inches in diameter. A lower degree of tree damage was |observed on the east side of the road. It is suspected that |additional damage extended further into the Green Swamp, |however the area was inaccessible.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain fell across the upper Ararat River basin during the early morning hours of November 12th. During a 3-hour time period, from 3:15 am to 6:15 am, the river rose about 8 feet and exceeded flood stage.  The river eventually crested at 15.75 feet during early afternoon, which is under a 10-year Average Return Interval per the USGS StreamStats website.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water on the road at the 9800 block of S Chicken Rd. High water was also reported at Turkey Branch Rd and Hwy 41.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and isolated thunderstorms were observed training across the foothills and across the Blue Ridge of North Carolina during the early morning hours of September 12th.  This convection developed in an environment of deep tropical moisture along the periphery of high pressure wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians.  Despite low instability, rainfall rates still managed to reach two inches per hour in a few spots.|Runoff from this rainfall resulted in the South Fork New River near Jefferson rising two feet between 2 am and 7 am EDT on the 12th and triggered a small debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a small debris flow to occur from a hillside onto Highway 221 in the community of Jefferson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across the Piedmont of North Carolina ahead of an approaching cold front, in an environment of moderate instability and strong wind shear. These storms produced damaging winds at several locations in Caswell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down one tree near the intersection of NC Highway 119 North and Stephentown Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced three tornadoes across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF0 tornado. A weak tornado impacted a small area from Indian Creek to Panther Swamp, just to the east of Indian Branch Road in the Panther Swamp area. The tornado mainly caused damage to trees and lifted before reaching Parker Road and nearby Route 158.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture and heavy rain overspread western North Carolina throughout the 28th through the morning of the 29th as the center of Tropical Storm Zeta moved quickly from the mouth of the Mississippi River into the southern Appalachians. Strong winds developed across portions of western North Carolina during the morning, downing hundreds of trees across the area. Multiple trees fell on structures and vehicles. Upslope flow into the Blue Ridge escarpment enhanced rainfall rates such that widespread totals of 3 to 5 with locally higher amounts inches occurred over about a 24 hour period. This resulted in development of flash flooding across portions of the southern mountains and northern foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain tapered off across the foothills during the morning and early afternoon, stream gauges indicated flooding persisted into the evening, mainly along the Johns River in response to persistent runoff from the earlier heavy rainfall. Multiple roads remained inundated along the river north of Morganton.

Upslope flow into the Blue Ridge escarpment enhanced rainfall rates such that widespread totals of 3 to 5 with locally higher amounts inches occurred over about a 24 hour period. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain tapered off across Caldwell County during the morning, stream gauges indicated continued runoff from the heavy rain resulted in high water conditions persisting along Johns River as well as Lower Creek in Lenoir through the afternoon. Multiple roads remained inundated, particularly in the Collettsville areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an area of low pressure and attendant cold front moved across western North Carolina throughout Christmas Eve into Christmas morning. Snow levels dropped quickly across the mountains during the evening hours, resulting in rain changing to snow. By the time the snow tapered off around daybreak on Christmas, snowfall totals range from 1 to 4 inches across the area, with locally higher amounts in the higher elevations. This was the first notable Christmas snowfall for this area since 2010.

Cold air filtering into western North Carolina on the heels of very gusty northwest winds in the wake of an Arctic cold front resulted in widespread temperatures in the teens throughout Christmas night into the morning of the 26th. Winds gusting in the 25 to 35 mph range resulted in wind chill values of -5 or colder, primarily in elevations above about 3000 feet. Meanwhile, elevations above 5000 feet or so saw gustier winds, temperatures in the single digits, and wind chill values of -15 or lower, with values as cold as -30 reported on the highest peaks and ridge tops.

An area of light freezing rain overspread the northwest Piedmont of central North Carolina during he morning of the 16th. Light freezing rain continued into the late morning and early afternoon, before changing over to all rain. The light freezing rain produced ice accrual amounts of around a tenth of an inch or less. The light freezing rain resulted in little to no impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace to one-tenth of an inch of ice from freezing rain was reported across the county, mainly in trees and along elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms intensified as it moved eastward across central North Carolina as an associated strong cold front moved across the area. As the line of convection moved across eastern portions of the area the line produced strong to damaging winds, resulting in some localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were reported down at South Pine Street and West 7th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms intensified as it moved eastward across central North Carolina as an associated strong cold front moved across the area. As the line of convection moved across eastern portions of the area the line produced strong to damaging winds, resulting in some localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down along United States Highway 70, near Shortwell Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms intensified as it moved eastward across central North Carolina as an associated strong cold front moved across the area. As the line of convection moved across eastern portions of the area the line produced strong to damaging winds, resulting in some localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were reported down, along with damage to vehicles and houses on North Carolina Highway 222.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias came off the African coast as a tropical wave on July 23, 2020. It became Tropical Storm Isaias on July 30th, further strengthening to a hurricane on the 31st. After crossing the Bahamas, it weakened to a tropical storm. Isaias strengthened again to a hurricane as it approached the coast of North Carolina. Hurricane Isaias made landfall at Ocean Isle Beach as a strong Category 1 around 11 pm on August 3rd, with an 87 mph recorded gust. It pushed inland to Goldsboro NC, where it weakened and accelerated northward. ||The hurricane spawned seven tornadoes, ranging from EF-0 to EF-2. Most of the tornadoes occurred in Brunswick County.||Rainfall was heaviest along the coast of Georgetown County, topping out at nearly seven inches. Most areas along the coast of North and South Carolina received around three to four inches on average, with lesser amounts inland. The Wilmington ASOS reported a gust to 74 mph. Storm surge was significant in Brunswick County, where many docks were damage, and homes and businesses were inundated with three feet of water.  Downtown Wilmington also received a record surge that came up the Cape Fear River. Over 80,000 residences lost power in New Hanover county, with widespread outages along the coast of Pender County, as they were in the eastern eyewall. Total damage was at least 300 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak tornado impacted a small area near Law Road |SE and Old Ocean Highway East near the Brunswick |County Government Complex.  The tornado mainly |caused damage to trees and lifted after crossing |Ocean Highway East (Highway 17).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced a squall line and numerous tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 ft tree was blown down on Milford Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture and heavy rain overspread western North Carolina throughout the 28th through the morning of the 29th as the center of Tropical Storm Zeta moved quickly from the mouth of the Mississippi River into the southern Appalachians. Strong winds developed across portions of western North Carolina during the morning, downing hundreds of trees across the area. Multiple trees fell on structures and vehicles. Upslope flow into the Blue Ridge escarpment enhanced rainfall rates such that widespread totals of 3 to 5 with locally higher amounts inches occurred over about a 24 hour period. This resulted in development of flash flooding across portions of the southern mountains and northern foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rainfall tapered off across the French Broad basin during the morning of the 29th, flooding continued along the Little River in response to continued runoff. Minor river flooding also developed along the French Broad in the Blantyre area during this time and continued into the early morning hours of November 1st.

Cold air filtering into western North Carolina on the heels of very gusty northwest winds in the wake of an Arctic cold front resulted in widespread temperatures in the teens throughout Christmas night into the morning of the 26th. Winds gusting in the 25 to 35 mph range resulted in wind chill values of -5 or colder, primarily in elevations above about 3000 feet. Meanwhile, elevations above 5000 feet or so saw gustier winds, temperatures in the single digits, and wind chill values of -15 or lower, with values as cold as -30 reported on the highest peaks and ridge tops.

A strong cold front swept through Eastern NC late Christmas Eve. Periods of showers and thunderstorms accompanied the cold front, with several storms becoming severe. Strong southerly wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph and minor coastal flooding accompanied the front as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a carport on Wyse Fork Rd was scattered as far as 300 yards from the house.  Additionally, a light pole near the property was broken and a home across the street sustained damage.  The time was estimated based on radar data.

Hurricane Zeta made landfall along the Louisiana coast during the afternoon of October 28th and quickly lift off to the north and northeast, moving across the southern Appalachians and southern Mid-Atlantic region on October 29th while transitioning to a extra-tropical cyclone. This resulted in numerous reports of wind damage across the area, especially across the northern Piedmont of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph resulted in scattered to numerous trees and power lines down across Montgomery County, including some on homes. Numerous customers lost power in Montgomery County as a result of the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light freezing rain overspread the northwest Piedmont of central North Carolina during he morning of the 16th. Light freezing rain continued into the late morning and early afternoon, before changing over to all rain. The light freezing rain produced ice accrual amounts of around a tenth of an inch or less. The light freezing rain resulted in little to no impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace to one-tenth of an inch of ice from freezing rain was reported across the county, mainly in trees and along elevated surfaces.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was spotted just off of Soles Road in Columbus |County at 8:10 am. After touching down at the corner of a |field, the tornado proceeded north through a wooded area. |Several agricultural sheds next to the the wooded area |along Soles Road were completely destroyed after being |recently renovated. Metal debris from the buildings was |scattered across Soles Road and down the length of |Tanglewood Acres Drive. Some metal debris was wrapped |around large diameter trees whose trunks were snapped or |uprooted. Damage indicates that the tornado began to lift |on the north side of Soles Road with the tops of trees |completely broken off. Damage continued down the length |of Tanglewood Drive with numerous trees and large branches |down. Residents reported downed power lines and roof damage.

Strong northwest winds pushing moisture up the western slopes of the Appalachians resulted in development of numerous to widespread snow showers near the Tennessee border. By midnight on the 1st, accumulations of up 2 inches were reported, with locally higher amounts above 3500 feet. The snow showers continued into the 1st, when heavy snowfall totals were realized during the pre-dawn hours.
Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across western North Carolina throughout the afternoon and evening. Several of the storms produced brief damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported (via Social Media) trees blown down on Painted Horse Dr and large limbs down on Secreat Short Cut Rd.

Hurricane Zeta made landfall along the Louisiana coast during the afternoon of October 28th and quickly lift off to the north and northeast, moving across the southern Appalachians and southern Mid-Atlantic region on October 29th while transitioning to a extra-tropical cyclone. This resulted in numerous reports of wind damage across the area, especially across the northern Piedmont of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph resulted in scattered to numerous trees and power lines down across Nash County, including some on homes. Numerous customers lost power in Nash County as a result of the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Isaias tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from late Monday night, August 3rd through Tuesday morning, August 4th. Outer bands north and east of the Tropical Storm produced three tornadoes across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the NWS Wakefield office found a path of storm damage consistent with an EF3 tornado. The tornado touched down near the intersection of Knowles Lane and Middle Track Road. It then tracked to the north northwest and reached its strongest point just south of the town of Woodard as it crossed Morning Road, where its width increased to just over one third of a mile and it reached EF3 intensity. The tornado completely destroyed several mobile homes and stick built houses in this area. It flattened seven single-wide and double-wide mobile homes, leaving unrecognizable bits of debris. The fatalities and injuries occurred in the vicinity of Morning Road. The tornado weakened to EF1 intensity as it approached the west side of the town of Windsor, but it did damage a farm on Highway 306. The tornado then lifted just before reaching Governors Road about 2 miles northwest of Windsor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rainfall tapered off across Rowan County during the afternoon and evening, elevated flow conditions and some flooding persisted across many of the larger basins in the city through much of the evening. Numerous roads remained closed during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Eta moved from the eastern Gulf of Mexico, across the northern Florida peninsula, to off the South Carolina coast throughout the 11th and 12th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the Catawba River at Lookout Shoals Dam indicated major flash flooding developed just upstream of the dam after 5 to 8 inches of rain fell throughout the Catawba basin, with the bulk of that falling in only a 3-hour period between midnight and sunrise on the 12th. Numerous homes were inundated and damaged, with numerous residents evacuated along the western shores of Lookout Shoals Lake. This was the second major flood event along this portion of the Catawba in less than two years (June 2019), and the second highest crest on record at Lookout Shoals (August 1940).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and resultant flash flooding occurred during the afternoon hours.  The rain was caused by a combination of a slow moving cold front along with moisture in connection with Tropical Storm Eta, which eventually would track across the northeastern Gulf of Mexico, across northern Florida, and well off of the NC coast.  While rain began the day before, on November 10th, the heaviest of the rain fell through the afternoon hours of November 11th.  Storm total rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 were common across much of Eastern NC, with 6 to 8 falling near areas such as Snow Hill, Kinston and northern Duplin County.  Numerous reports of flash flooding occurred around the area.  In addition to the heavy rain, in the heavier showers it was quite breezy with winds of 35 to 45 MPH quite common. In exposed areas near or over the water the wind was stronger, with many places gusting over 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in and around Faison.  Numerous fields around town were submerged.  Nearby Highways 403 and 50 were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stream gauges reported flash flooding developed along the major headwater streams in Transylvania County after 4 to 5 inches of rain fell across much of the southern and western part of the county in around 24 hours. The Little River overflowed its banks and flooded Merrill Rd in the eastern part of the county. The French Broad River inundated multiple roads in the Rosman area, including Depot St, where some homes has to be evacuated, as well as Hannah Ford Rd, E Main St, and Highway 178. The Davidson River also overflowed its banks in the Pisgah Forest area, flooding portions of Davidson River Rd and Wilson Rd, and indicating backwater flooding of Turkey Creek which inundated Deavor Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flash flooding developed along portions of the Catawba River and many of its tributaries across central McDowell County after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell in around 24 hours. In addition to around two dozen roads being inundated and closed, a water rescue occurred at an apartment complex in Old Fort when Mill Creek overflowed its banks. Private bridges were destroyed by intense stream flow along Second Broad River at Mimosa Dr and Clear Creek Rd (both in the Marion area) and along Peppers Creek Rd in North Cove. A culvert under Hidden Forest Drive was also washed out by a tributary of Limekiln Creek in Woodlawn. Additionally, a stream gauge on the Catawba River exceeded its established flood stage in the Pleasant Gardens community, indicating inundation of a campground off North Main St just north of Marion.

Precipitation associated with an area of low pressure and attendant cold front moved across western North Carolina throughout Christmas Eve into Christmas morning. Snow levels dropped quickly across the mountains during the evening hours, resulting in rain changing to snow. By the time the snow tapered off around daybreak on Christmas, snowfall totals range from 1 to 4 inches across the area, with locally higher amounts in the higher elevations. This was the first notable Christmas snowfall for this area since 2010.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rainfall tapered off across Alexander County throughout the morning, runoff from the earlier excessive rain continued to cause elevated stream levels and localized high water conditions to persist into the afternoon. A portion of a bridge over the South Yadkin collapsed on Cheatham Ford Rd during this time. Part of another bridge was washed out on Hopewell Church Rd (over Mill Creek). A 64-year-old man was killed when he drove his vehicle over the collapsed bridge. At least two other bridges were also compromised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Eta moved from the eastern Gulf of Mexico, across the northern Florida peninsula, to off the South Carolina coast throughout the 11th and 12th. Tropical moisture streaming into the Carolinas throughout this time resulted in development of heavy rainfall, with widespread rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches, with locally higher amounts across the foothills and Piedmont of North Carolina. This resulted in areas of flash flooding and main stem river flooding, some of which was significant, resulting in multiple fatalities and significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported major flash flooding developed throughout Alexander County after up to 9 inches of rain fell in 24 hours, with around half of that falling in a 3-hour period prior to daybreak on the 12th. The most significant flooding occurred along the South Yadkin River in the Hiddenite area. Multiple recreational vehicles and campers were swept down the river from a campground off Princess Ln. Five people, ages ranging from 1 to 76, drowned and 31 more people required rescue from rapidly rising flood water. Otherwise, most of the creeks and streams in the central and northeast part of the county overflowed onto at least one road, with more than 50 roads closed at some point during the event.

Johns River inundated multiple roads, including Johns River Loop and Johns River Rd. A water rescue occurred on Johns River Loop. Lower Creek also flooded Antioch Rd near Highway 18. Pax Hill Rd and Hartland Rd also flooded in the Chesterfield community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread amounts of three to five inches of rainfall were observed across the mountains and foothills during the 24-hour period ending with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta across the lower mid-Atlantic region.  Despite its rapid speed across the region, the storm brought two to three inches of rain across portions of Wilkes and Surry Counties during a single hour, resulting in widespread flooding of many creeks and streams.  The flooding was severe enough in some cases to wash out the bridges passing over these creeks. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread amounts of three to five inches of rainfall were observed across the mountains and foothills during the 24-hour period ending with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta across the lower mid-Atlantic region.  Despite its rapid speed across the region, the storm brought two to three inches of rain across portions of Wilkes and Surry Counties during a single hour, resulting in widespread flooding of many creeks and streams.  The flooding was severe enough in some cases to wash out the bridges passing over these creeks.  Lesser flooding was observed elsewhere across the Highlands of North Carolina where the rainfall rates were not as intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding took place along Cub Creek during the late morning and afternoon hours as two to three inches of rain fell across the basin with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta, most of it within a one hour period, on top of an additional one to two inches that fell during the previous evening for a total of nearly five inches in 24 hours.  Virtually all of Cub Creek Park was flooded, and the bridge on South Bridge Street that crosses the creek was completely over-topped by the flooded creek.  Elsewhere, the dam of a 3.5 acre pond collapsed at around 1:40 pm EST, sending flood water that washed out at 20 foot section of Woodfield Way just downstream.  Concern for the bridge on Industrial Park Road that crosses the creek about one mile downstream prompted evacuations of multiple businesses and apartments that would be cut off if the that bridge too was washed out, though it did not. Country Club Extension Road along Little Cub Creek was flooded and closed, as was Westover Road, where storage buildings and nearby residences were also flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding took place along Moravian Creek during the late morning and afternoon hours as 2 to 3 inches of rain fell across this basin with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta, most of it within a 1-hour period.  This rain was in addition to another 1 to 2 inches that fell during the previous evening, for a total of nearly 5 inches in 24 hours.  Newspaper reports indicate that nearly all of Moravian Creek was out of its banks, causing flooding Wilkes Community College's lower campus.  School Street further downstream was also closed by the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread amounts of three to five inches of rainfall were observed across the mountains and foothills during the 24-hour period ending with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta across the lower mid-Atlantic region.  Despite its rapid speed across the region, the storm brought two to three inches of rain across portions of Wilkes and Surry Counties during a single hour, resulting in widespread flooding of many creeks and streams. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous businesses along Wilkesboro Boulevard from the Yadkin River bridge to Memorial Park were flooded when the Yadkin River swiftly rose out of its banks, the result of 4 to 5 inches or rain within a 24-hour period both preceding and during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta. Multiple personal weather stations observed 2-3 inches of rain within a 1-hour period just before the onset of flooding. Flash flooding ended as the river crested during mid-afternoon, and roads were reopened during the evening once the river receded. Among the businesses flooded were Extreme Collision Auto Body, which had to move several vehicles to prevent them from being damaged by flood waters. Flood waters completely inundated Memorial Park as well as the Yadkin River Greenway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread amounts of three to five inches of rainfall were observed across the mountains and foothills during the 24-hour period ending with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta across the lower mid-Atlantic region. The flooding was severe enough in some cases to wash out the bridges passing over these creeks.  Lesser flooding was observed elsewhere across the Highlands of North Carolina where the rainfall rates were not as intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Reddies River at Wilkesboro (REDN7) crested at 11.79 feet (6760 cfs), which was between a 5- and 10-year Annual Return Interval per USGS StreamStats.  These rises were the result of 3-5 inches of rainfall within a 24-hour period, much of which fell within a 2-hour period during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta.  The rapid rises resulted in the precautionary evacuation of residents from the Rose Glen Manor assisted living facility, though flooding did not enter the buildings.  Employees of other nearby businesses were evacuated as well out of precaution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread amounts of three to five inches of rainfall were observed across the mountains and foothills during the 24-hour period ending with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta across the lower mid-Atlantic region.  Despite its rapid speed across the region, the storm brought two to three inches of rain across portions of Wilkes and Surry Counties during a single hour, resulting in widespread flooding of many creeks and streams.  The flooding was severe enough in some cases to wash out the bridges passing over these creeks.  Lesser flooding was observed elsewhere across the Highlands of North Carolina where the rainfall rates were not as intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moravian Creek quickly rose out of its banks during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta, the result of three to five inches of rain within a 24-hour period.  This caused several roads intersecting the creek to be flooded and closed until the flood water receded, including Germantown Road and Falls Road near the Moravian Falls waterfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yadkin River at Wilkesboro (WKSN7) crested within minor flood stage at 15.86 feet (13,100 cfs), resulting from heavy rain during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta.  This crest is between a 2-year and a 5-year annual return interval per USGS StreamStats.  The crest would likely have been higher had W. Kerr Scott Reservoir not reduced its outflow by 500 cfs in response to the flooding at several points downstream.  By holding back outflow and combined with heavy inflow, W. Kerr Scott Reservoir had risen to a pool stage of 1044 feet by 9 pm on October 29th, up 14 feet from before the onset of rainfall during the day prior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper-level low combined with a surge of warmer and more moist air that moved over a cooler air mass near the surface produced multiple rounds of rain, showers, and thunderstorms across the Coastal Plain and the northeast Piedmont of North Carolina during the morning hours. Widespread rain fell across the area during the previous day or two saturating the soil and raising stream and creek levels. A narrow corridor of heavy rain moved across this area overnight and during the morning hours and produced the flash flooding. Several locations reported between 5 and 6 inches of rain. Many roads were flooded, with at least one water rescue and some roads damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall measured 5.39 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread amounts of three to five inches of rainfall were observed across the mountains and foothills during the 24-hour period ending with the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta across the lower mid-Atlantic region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 4 inches of rainfall occurred in the hours before and during the passage of Tropical Storm Zeta. Multiple personal weather stations indicate that more than half of the rain fell within a 2-hour period as the center of the storm passed. The heavy rain caused widespread flooding of small creeks and streams, pushing many out of their banks. Several roads, both within the Elkin community and the surrounding area, were flooded and impassible until flooding receded, including Commerce Street, Pleasant Hill Road, and Memorial Park Drive. A debris flow was reported on Johnson Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple mid to upper level disturbances moving through the region triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms. One cluster of storms moved across the northern Piedmont counties, knocking down a few trees in Burlington, including one tree down onto a home. The same cluster of storms continued to move east across Orange County where an isolated tree crashed through a mobile home and caused a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on a house, along with multiple trees reported down in Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front initiated scattered thunderstorms across Virginia and North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. The convection initially developed across the northern Piedmont and then back built to the west as the overall line of storms moved south. The slow moving nature of the storms combined with their back-building nature also resulted in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles were reported down near Tuscarora Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from Tropical Storm Eta was drawn northward into the Mid Atlantic during the day of Wednesday November 11th. Meanwhile, a slow moving cold front approached the area and made its way through central North Carolina during the day Thursday, resulting in widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms. Rainfall totals were generally in the 4 to 6 inches range, although some spots near Rocky Mount saw close to 10 inches of rain by the time the event drew to a close. This resulted in numerous flooded road, including one death resulting from the flash flooding. One thunderstorm also caused some localized storm damage in Sampson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Fountain Drive, where Walnut Creek had overflowed it���s banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias made landfall in southeast North Carolina, then moved north through coastal North Carolina. Significant rain fell across the Coastal Plain, Sandhills and eastern Piedmont of North Carolina as Isaias moved northward through the state along with gusty winds up to 50 to 60 miles per hour. Isaias produced two to five inches of rain across much of eastern NC, resulting in flash flooding, as well as minor flooding along the Neuse River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall for Tropical Cyclone Isaias was 3.93 inches.

Rainfall totals were generally in the 4 to 6 inches range, although some spots near Rocky Mount saw close to 10 inches of rain by the time the event drew to a close. This resulted in numerous flooded road, including one death resulting from the flash flooding. One thunderstorm also caused some localized storm damage in Sampson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Rex Road, between Avent Ferry Road and Cass Holt Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm moist air lifted along a wavy stationary front. This combined with slow moving and merging thunderstorms resulted in heavy rain and flash flooding, including a dam break in Johnston County and two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding along Galilee Road. Part of the road washed away in the flooded waters, which resulted in a car being swept off the road. Two individuals were not able to be rescued and drowned in the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias made landfall in southeast North Carolina, then moved north through coastal North Carolina. Significant rain fell across the Coastal Plain, Sandhills and eastern Piedmont of North Carolina as Isaias moved northward through the state along with gusty winds up to 50 to 60 miles per hour. Isaias produced two to five inches of rain across much of eastern NC, resulting in flash flooding, as well as minor flooding along the Neuse River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall for Tropical Cyclone Isaias was 3.56 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along a southward sinking outflow boundary in a very moist atmosphere, characterized by a weak westerly steering flow. This resulted in training showers and storms producing heavy rainfall. This resulted in several reports of flash flooding across the Piedmont of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding at Old Town Drive and Bethabara Road resulted in a water rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant low from Tropical Cyclone Beta was moving from north Alabama into Georgia, along a warm frontal zone across South Carolina and south-central North Carolina to the northern Outer Banks. Meanwhile, a mid level shortwave trough was tracking eastward across southwestern Virginia. Convection began to increase in a broad band just south of central North Carolina, and it was from this that two distinct cells started accelerating to the north-northeastward, with the leftmost cell eventually becoming dominant and intensifying over northeast Lee County as it move to the of the surface boundary. It produced a swath of large hail across western Wake County before starting to weaken over far northwest Wake County.

Meanwhile, a slow moving cold front approached the area and made its way through central North Carolina during the day Thursday, resulting in widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms. Rainfall totals were generally in the 4 to 6 inches range, although some spots near Rocky Mount saw close to 10 inches of rain by the time the event drew to a close. This resulted in numerous flooded road, including one death resulting from the flash flooding. One thunderstorm also caused some localized storm damage in Sampson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Highway 52 in Albemarle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias made landfall in southeast North Carolina, then moved north through coastal North Carolina. Significant rain fell across the Coastal Plain, Sandhills and eastern Piedmont of North Carolina as Isaias moved northward through the state along with gusty winds up to 50 to 60 miles per hour. Isaias produced two to five inches of rain across much of eastern NC, resulting in flash flooding, as well as minor flooding along the Neuse River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall for Tropical Cyclone Isaias was 5.06 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant low from Tropical Cyclone Beta was moving from north Alabama into Georgia, along a warm frontal zone across South Carolina and south-central North Carolina to the northern Outer Banks. Meanwhile, a mid level shortwave trough was tracking eastward across southwestern Virginia. Convection began to increase in a broad band just south of central North Carolina, and it was from this that two distinct cells started accelerating to the north-northeastward, with the leftmost cell eventually becoming dominant and intensifying over northeast Lee County as it move to the of the surface boundary. It produced a swath of large hail across western Wake County before starting to weaken over far northwest Wake County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along a southward sinking outflow boundary in a very moist atmosphere, characterized by a weak westerly steering flow. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles were reported stuck in flood waters at the intersection of North Market Street and Craftsman Drive.
