Meanwhile, a slow moving cold front approached the area and made its way through central North Carolina during the day Thursday, resulting in widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms. Rainfall totals were generally in the 4 to 6 inches range, although some spots near Rocky Mount saw close to 10 inches of rain by the time the event drew to a close. This resulted in numerous flooded road, including one death resulting from the flash flooding. One thunderstorm also caused some localized storm damage in Sampson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Railroad Street, between North Carolina Highway 222 and the Wilson County Line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant low from Tropical Cyclone Beta was moving from north Alabama into Georgia, along a warm frontal zone across South Carolina and south-central North Carolina to the northern Outer Banks. Meanwhile, a mid level shortwave trough was tracking eastward across southwestern Virginia. Convection began to increase in a broad band just south of central North Carolina, and it was from this that two distinct cells started accelerating to the north-northeastward, with the leftmost cell eventually becoming dominant and intensifying over northeast Lee County as it move to the of the surface boundary. It produced a swath of large hail across western Wake County before starting to weaken over far northwest Wake County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down near the intersection of Leesville Road and Crestmont Drive.

Convection began to increase in a broad band just south of central North Carolina, and it was from this that two distinct cells started accelerating to the north-northeastward, with the leftmost cell eventually becoming dominant and intensifying over northeast Lee County as it move to the of the surface boundary. It produced a swath of large hail across western Wake County before starting to weaken over far northwest Wake County.

Moisture from Tropical Storm Eta was drawn northward into the Mid Atlantic during the day of Wednesday November 11th. Meanwhile, a slow moving cold front approached the area and made its way through central North Carolina during the day Thursday, resulting in widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms. Rainfall totals were generally in the 4 to 6 inches range, although some spots near Rocky Mount saw close to 10 inches of rain by the time the event drew to a close. This resulted in numerous flooded road, including one death resulting from the flash flooding. One thunderstorm also caused some localized storm damage in Sampson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported at Mary Chapel Road near Scotland Neck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Isaias made landfall in southeast North Carolina, then moved north through coastal North Carolina. Significant rain fell across the Coastal Plain, Sandhills and eastern Piedmont of North Carolina as Isaias moved northward through the state along with gusty winds up to 50 to 60 miles per hour. Isaias produced two to five inches of rain across much of eastern NC, resulting in flash flooding, as well as minor flooding along the Neuse River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall for Tropical Cyclone Isaias was 3.24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked northeast along the east coast of the U.S. Moisture from this system fell as a wintry mix across parts of North Carolina. Freezing rain impacted the Northern Mountains and Foothill region of North Carolina with ice accretions averaging around one-tenth of an inch to as much as four-tenths of an inch. At the higher end of this range, specific amounts included three-tenths at near Sparta, one-quarter inch near Baldwin, one-quarter inch near Aho, and four-tenths of an inch at North Wilkesboro. Light amounts of sleet and/or snow also fell during this event. Key Impacts: Slick and slushy roads and sidewalks, downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion totals include four-tenths at North Wilkesboro. Snow and/or sleet amounts were very light. Key Impacts: Slick and slushy roads and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light freezing rain overspread the northwest Piedmont of central North Carolina during he morning of the 16th. Light freezing rain continued into the late morning and early afternoon, before changing over to all rain. The light freezing rain produced ice accrual amounts of around a tenth of an inch or less. The light freezing rain resulted in little to no impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace to one-tenth of an inch of ice from freezing rain was reported across the county, mainly in trees and along elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an area of low pressure and attendant cold front moved across western North Carolina throughout Christmas Eve into Christmas morning. Snow levels dropped quickly across the mountains during the evening hours, resulting in rain changing to snow. While much of the accumulating snow ended by around sunrise on Christmas Day, continuing snow showers produced additional light snowfall amounts throughout the day, especially in the higher elevations along state line. Snowfall totals were generally in the 3 to 6 inch range, although amounts of around a foot were reported in the higher elevations, and even in portions of the lower French Broad Valley. This was the first Christmas heavy snow event to impact western North Carolina since 2010.

Precipitation associated with an area of low pressure and attendant cold front moved across western North Carolina throughout Christmas Eve into Christmas morning. Snow levels dropped quickly across the mountains during the evening hours, resulting in rain changing to snow. While much of the accumulating snow ended by around sunrise on Christmas Day, continuing snow showers produced additional light snowfall amounts throughout the day, especially in the higher elevations along state line. Snowfall totals were generally in the 3 to 6 inch range, although amounts of around a foot were reported in the higher elevations, and even in portions of the lower French Broad Valley. This was the first Christmas heavy snow event to impact western North Carolina since 2010.

Light freezing rain developed over portions of western North Carolina, primarily areas along the eastern Blue Ridge escarpment, the northern foothills, and northwest Piedmont as areas of low pressure moved along the Gulf and southeast Atlantic coast. The precipitation changed to rain in most locations during the late morning as temperatures warmed above freezing. Total ice accretion was generally around a tenth of an inch or less, although isolated spots along the eastern Blue Ridge saw around a quarter of an inch.

An area of low pressure raced eastward across the mid-Atlantic region early on the morning of the 21st. On the south side of this system, winds increased across the higher terrain of northwest North Carolina, topping at 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 MPH wind gust was measured in Boone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure raced eastward across the mid-Atlantic region early on the morning of the 21st. On the south side of this system, winds increased across the higher terrain of northwest North Carolina, topping at 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust was measured by North Carolina's ECONet observing system at Mt. Jefferson. A 60 mph wind gust was measured by a personal weather station two miles East-Northeast of Baldwin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across the region during the evening hours of Christmas Eve. Along and advance of the cold front, very gusty winds occurred. Some were strong enough to down trees in parts of Stokes and Watauga Counties and blow off a roof in Watauga County. While rain preceded the cold front, sub-freezing air rapidly entered the area after the passage of the cold front, dropping the temperatures some five to eight degrees per hour. This quickly turned the rain to snow, and resulted in flash freeze of surface moisture to slippery ice. The snow quickly accumulated during the night, with many areas of the mountains of North Carolina receiving four to seven inches of snow. Key impacts: icy roads and sidewalks, falling trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds caused trees to be uprooted or snapped and blew off a roof one mile south of Lovill. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an area of low pressure and attendant cold front moved across western North Carolina throughout Christmas Eve into Christmas morning. Snow levels dropped quickly across the mountains during the evening hours, resulting in rain changing to snow. By the time the snow tapered off around daybreak on Christmas, snowfall totals range from 1 to 4 inches across the area, with locally higher amounts in the higher elevations. This was the first notable Christmas snowfall for this area since 2010.

A potent shortwave disturbance aloft and associated cold front allowed for the development of a damaging line of showers and storms. The prefrontal line moved across the area during the late evening and into the early morning hours, producing lots of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of fasting moving showers and storms tracked across the far eastern Sandhills and southern Coastal Plain of central North Carolina during the early to mid afternoon in a highly sheared, but weak instability environment. The cluster of storms became strong enough to produced some localized wind damage, including some significant wind damage to Union Intermediate Elementary School in Sampson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a structure was reported at Baker Chapel Church Road and Thunder Swap Road.
